region_of_origin: China (Cantonese / Southern Chinese tradition); widely served in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a pea shoot food product class (FOODON_03311737); no specific stir-fried-with-garlic preparation subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cooked pea pods and shoots. No garlic-stir-fried-specific code exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for stir-fried pea shoots with garlic as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of stir-fried dou miao with garlic is approximately 200 g, presented as a full plate of tender pea shoots lightly seasoned with garlic and oil.

nutrient_source.title: Pea shoots, cooked (boiled / stir-fried), per 100 g — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 11304 adapted for stir-fried preparation with oil
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked pea shoots (NDB 11304, peas, edible-podded, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt) used as the primary nutrient anchor. Values adjusted upward for fat and sodium to reflect stir-frying with approximately 5 ml vegetable oil per 100 g cooked weight and light salt seasoning, consistent with standard Chinese restaurant stir-fry preparation. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors. This is the best available approved-source match; no Singapore HPB Foodo entry for dou miao stir-fry was located.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of non-starchy leafy green vegetables — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for cooked green vegetables (GI range 10–20)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for stir-fried dou miao (pea shoots) with garlic was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 15 based on the database's published values for cooked non-starchy green vegetables (e.g., green beans GI ~15, cooked spinach GI ~15). Pea shoots are a low-carbohydrate leafy vegetable with high fibre content, supporting a low GI classification. Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as (15 × 5.2 × 2.0) / 100 = 1.56, rounded to 1.6.

hypertension.guidance: At approximately 180 mg sodium per 100 g, a 200 g restaurant serving contributes around 360 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should request reduced salt and sauce and monitor total daily sodium intake, keeping this dish within a low-sodium meal plan.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low net carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 15 and only 5.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, stir-fried dou miao with garlic has a negligible glycemic load and is an excellent vegetable choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, supporting blood glucose control when consumed as part of a balanced meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High dietary fibre, low saturated fat, and beneficial micronutrients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Stir-fried dou miao provides dietary fibre (2.6 g/100 g), folate, potassium, and vitamin C with minimal saturated fat (0.4 g/100 g) and zero cholesterol, making it a heart-healthy vegetable dish suitable for cardiovascular disease prevention when prepared with minimal oil and salt.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Dou Miao with Garlic (蒜炒豆苗, Suàn Chǎo Dòu Miáo) is a classic Chinese vegetable dish featuring young, tender pea shoots (dou miao) rapidly wok-fried over high heat with fragrant garlic and a small amount of vegetable oil. Dou miao refers to the delicate young shoots and leaves of the snow pea or sugar snap pea plant (Pisum sativum), prized in Chinese cuisine for their sweet, fresh flavour, silky yet slightly crisp texture, and vibrant green colour. The dish is a staple of Cantonese and broader Chinese restaurant menus across Singapore, Hong Kong, and mainland China, and is particularly associated with premium Chinese dining establishments such as Din Tai Fung. The preparation is intentionally minimal: the shoots are blanched briefly in the wok with hot oil and crushed or sliced garlic, seasoned with salt, and served immediately to preserve their delicate texture and bright colour. The result is a dish that is simultaneously simple and refined. Nutritionally, stir-fried dou miao is a low-calorie, low-carbohydrate, high-fibre vegetable dish providing approximately 62 kcal per 100 g. It is rich in folate, vitamin C, vitamin A, iron, and potassium, and contains negligible saturated fat and zero cholesterol. Its very low glycemic index (estimated GI ~15) makes it an excellent choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The moderate sodium content from stir-fry seasoning warrants attention for hypertensive individuals. The dish is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and halal, making it broadly suitable across dietary requirements. It is typically served as a shared vegetable side dish in a Chinese meal context.

Healthy vegetable accompaniment to rice or noodles

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry for stir-fried dou miao with garlic was located. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked pea shoot data with adjustments for stir-fry oil and salt addition. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database values for comparable cooked non-starchy green vegetables. Confidence is medium rather than low because the adaptation is methodologically sound and the source data is from an approved primary database.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from input value 'Grains and staples' to 'Vegetables', which is the correct classification for a leafy green vegetable dish. 2. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (cooked pea shoots / edible-podded peas) with upward adjustment for stir-fry oil (~5 ml/100 g cooked) and light salt seasoning. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors: protein 3.5 g × 4 = 14 kcal, fat 3.2 g × 9 = 28.8 kcal, carbohydrate 5.2 g × 4 = 20.8 kcal; total ~63.6 kcal, rounded to 62 kcal. 3. GI estimated at 15 (low) based on analogous cooked non-starchy green vegetables in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement found for dou miao. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (15 × 5.2 × 2.0) / 100 = 1.56, rounded to 1.6. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): pea shoots are a plant food containing no vitamin B12 by nature — naturally zero. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): pea shoots contain no meaningful vitamin D — naturally zero. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): vegetable oil used in stir-frying contains negligible trans fat — confirmed zero per USDA source data. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients — naturally zero. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g estimated from typical vegetable oil fatty acid profile (sunflower/canola blend). 6. Serving size of 200 g is an estimate for a restaurant portion; actual Din Tai Fung portion weight may vary. 7. FoodOn URI FOODON_03311737 and FoodEx2 code A00LC are closest-parent matches; no exact stir-fried-pea-shoot-with-garlic codes exist in either ontology. 8. Cuisine tags from input (Dim Sum, Noodles) are not applicable to this dish and have been excluded from the output record.
